metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the routing pattern: either (a) follow the pure intercept model like `review-plan` (no `.txt`, no `individual` entry, no goldens, intercept fires before `_dispatch`) OR (b) add a real `_dispatch` arm (add to a frozenset or an explicit `if sub ==` in `_dispatch`, create `.txt`, add to `individual`, generate goldens) — the plan currently specifies both halves of two different patterns simultaneously.
findings.finding: The plan says `verify-commit-ticket` should 'mirror the `review-plan` intercept' in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py`, but then also requires creating `src/rebar/_cli/help/verify-commit-ticket.txt`. The `review-plan` intercept pattern has NO `.txt` file in the help dir and is NOT in `individual`. If the `.txt` file is created, `_help.known_subcommands()` will include `verify-commit-ticket`, but `_dispatch()` will not handle it (the intercept fires before `_dispatch` in `main()`). This causes `test_routable_set_matches_pinned_help_set` to FAIL with 'pinned help text but not routable'. The plan says to fix this by adding it to the `individual` set — but `individual` lists arms that `_dispatch()` actually handles, not intercepts. The plan's AC is internally contradictory: it cannot simultaneously be a pure intercept (like review-plan) AND be registered via the `.txt`/`individual` path without also adding a dispatch arm in `_dispatch()`.
findings.location: AC: Subcommand registration (all four touchpoints); `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py`; `src/rebar/_cli/help/verify-commit-ticket.txt`; `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_help_overview_coverage.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Since the plan wants help text, overview entry, and goldens (the full dispatch-registered subcommand model), add a `_VERIFY_COMMIT = frozenset({'verify-commit-ticket'})` to `_cli/__init__.py`, route it in `_dispatch()`, and remove the 'mirroring the review-plan intercept' description. This makes it consistent with the .txt/individual/golden AC items.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the registration conflict: verify whether `verify-commit-ticket` should follow the true intercept pattern (no `help/<sub>.txt`, not in `individual`, no help goldens — matching `review-plan`/`verify-completion`) or the `_dispatch` pattern (`.txt` + `individual` + goldens + an arm in `_dispatch`); the plan currently mandates both paths simultaneously, which the existing parity test will reject.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `verify-commit-ticket` should be added to the `individual` set in `test_help_overview_coverage.py` and that 6 golden help files (`verify-commit-ticket__{dashhelp,help}.{out,err,exit}`) should be generated — but the established codebase pattern for intercept commands (review-plan, verify-completion, workflow, llm, jira-onboard, prompt, criteria, config) is that they are NOT in `individual`, have NO pinned `help/<sub>.txt`, and have NO help golden files, because they own their own argparse `--help` and bypass the dispatcher's pinned-help machinery. The `known_subcommands()` scan (which feeds the parity test) is keyed to `.txt` presence — so adding a `.txt` for an intercept arm is inconsistent with every existing intercept arm's treatment and may break the parity test's implicit assumption that all `.txt`-registered commands are reachable via `_dispatch`.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — subcommand registration bullet (c)/(d) and the golden-files requirement
findings.suggested_fix: Follow one of the two existing patterns consistently. Option A (intercept, like review-plan): drop the `.txt` file, do NOT add to `individual`, generate NO help goldens — the argparse `--help` is self-contained. Option B (_dispatch, like `grounding-info`): add `.txt`, add to `individual`, add to `_dispatch` with a routing arm, generate goldens. Given the command 'owns its own --help' (plan text), Option A matches the established intercept prior art.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one proving command (e.g. `grep -q 'rebar-ticket' CONTRIBUTING.md CLAUDE.md docs/config.md`) to the Verification section for the non-trailer doc additions, or extend the doc-drift test to cover the key phrases in those files.
findings.finding: The Verification section provides no proving command for the CONTRIBUTING.md, CLAUDE.md, and docs/config.md additions. The doc-drift test only covers `docs/commit-ticket-trailer.md` ↔ `EXPECTED_FORMAT`; the other three doc files have no in-session check.
findings.location: Verification section; Docs AC ('CONTRIBUTING.md — every commit to main must carry…'; 'CLAUDE.md — in the ## Git workflow section…'; 'docs/config.md — the verify.require_ticket_for_commit flag')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Verification section: `grep -q 'rebar-ticket:' CONTRIBUTING.md CLAUDE.md && grep -q 'require_ticket_for_commit' docs/config.md`

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: Destructive/irreversible operation(s) with no stated safeguard.
findings.suggested_fix: State the safeguard (backup, dry-run, reversible migration, rollback) or justify the irreversibility explicitly (T4).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the three security-filter regexes in `extract_ticket_refs` reuse (import) existing canonical ID-shape constants from the resolver/shared module, or are newly introduced — if newly introduced, document them as the authoritative definition to prevent dual-maintenance drift.
findings.finding: The security-filter regexes in `extract_ticket_refs` (full/short hex, alias, Jira key) are specified inline in the AC without clarifying whether they are derived from or shared with any existing canonical ID-shape definitions elsewhere in the codebase. If rebar already defines these shapes (e.g., in the ticket resolver or a shared constants module), duplicating them creates a silent drift risk when new ID formats are introduced — the security filter could lag behind the resolver.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — `src/rebar/_commands/verify_commit.py`, `extract_ticket_refs` SECURITY bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Define the three regexes as module-level constants in a shared location (e.g., the existing ticket-resolver module or a `_ids.py` constants file) and have both `extract_ticket_refs` and `resolve_ticket_id` import them, so a format change is a single edit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Export (or publicly re-expose) `_FULL_ID_RE`, `_SHORT_ID_RE`, and `_JIRA_KEY_RE` from `src/rebar/_engine_support/resolver.py` and import them in `verify_commit.py`'s `extract_ticket_refs`, rather than redefining inline shapes — eliminating the parallel definition and the `{1,3}` combined-shape divergence.
findings.finding: The plan specifies three inline security-filter regexes in `extract_ticket_refs` that duplicate logic already defined as private constants in `src/rebar/_engine_support/resolver.py` (`_FULL_ID_RE`, `_SHORT_ID_RE`, `_JIRA_KEY_RE`), introducing a NIH parallel implementation with a concrete shape divergence: the plan's combined full/short pattern `^[a-z0-9]{4}(-[a-z0-9]{4}){1,3}$` collapses full and short IDs into one expression using `{1,3}`, while the resolver uses two separate patterns — meaning a 2-group or 3-group hex candidate accepted by the plan's filter would fail the resolver, silently inconsistent rather than co-defined.
findings.location: AC — `verify_commit.py`: `extract_ticket_refs` security filter shapes
findings.suggested_fix: In `resolver.py`, rename or alias the three regexes as public module-level exports (e.g. `FULL_ID_RE`, `SHORT_ID_RE`, `JIRA_KEY_RE`) and import them in `verify_commit.py`'s `extract_ticket_refs` for the security filter. The alias-shape `^[a-z][a-z0-9-]{2,}$` is not currently defined in `resolver.py` and would remain local.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the 6 golden files are only needed if `verify-commit-ticket` is registered via a `_dispatch` arm (not the intercept pattern); remove this golden-capture requirement from the AC if the intercept pattern is chosen instead.
findings.finding: The plan's AC for subcommand registration says '(b) `src/rebar/_cli/help/verify-commit-ticket.txt` (help text; `_help.py` LOADS these — `known_subcommands()` scans this dir, so the file's presence registers the command)'. This is accurate for the `_dispatch` path, but the plan says the golden capture needs 6 files (`tests/golden/cli_help/verify-commit-ticket__{dashhelp,help}.{out,err,exit}`). Looking at all other subcommands in `tests/golden/cli_help/`, the correct pattern is exactly 6 per subcommand. However, the `reconcile` intercept has a `reconcile__dashhelp.{err,exit,out}` set but NO `reconcile__help.{err,exit,out}` — this is an inconsistency in the plan's golden count if `verify-commit-ticket` truly mirrors the `review-plan` intercept (which has zero goldens). The '6 golden files' claim is consistent only with the full `_dispatch` registration pattern, further confirming the intercept/dispatch pattern conflict identified above.
findings.location: AC: Subcommand registration — 'regenerate the 6 golden files `tests/golden/cli_help/verify-commit-ticket__{dashhelp,help}.{out,err,exit}`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten the security-filter hex regex to exactly match the two shapes `resolve_ticket_id` recognises: `^[a-z0-9]{4}-[a-z0-9]{4}$` (short) OR `^[a-z0-9]{4}-[a-z0-9]{4}-[a-z0-9]{4}-[a-z0-9]{4}$` (full), rather than the combined `{1,3}` pattern that also passes 12-hex IDs.
findings.finding: The plan's security-filter regex for full/short hex IDs is `^[a-z0-9]{4}(-[a-z0-9]{4}){1,3}$`, which matches 8-hex, 12-hex, AND 16-hex forms. However, the actual `resolver.py` only recognises `_FULL_ID_RE = ^[a-z0-9]{4}-[a-z0-9]{4}-[a-z0-9]{4}-[a-z0-9]{4}$` (16-hex) and `_SHORT_ID_RE = ^[a-z0-9]{4}-[a-z0-9]{4}$` (8-hex). A 12-hex candidate (`abcd-1234-ef56`) would pass the plan's security filter and be handed to `resolve_ticket_id`, which would then fall through to the alias scanner — potentially triggering an expensive full-store alias scan on a value that cannot be a valid ticket ID.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — `extract_ticket_refs` SECURITY filter: `^[a-z0-9]{4}(-[a-z0-9]{4}){1,3}$`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the combined pattern with two disjoint patterns (or an explicit alternation): `^[a-z0-9]{4}-[a-z0-9]{4}$` and `^[a-z0-9]{4}-[a-z0-9]{4}-[a-z0-9]{4}-[a-z0-9]{4}$`, matching `_SHORT_ID_RE` and `_FULL_ID_RE` exactly.

findings.checklist_item:
- [ ] Specify (and test) the error behavior for subprocess/I/O failures in each input mode: `--rev ` when `git show` fails (non-zero exit or empty output), and `--message-file

` when the file is missing or unreadable — both should emit a clear, distinguishable error message and exit non-zero (not a raw traceback).
findings.finding:
The CI job does not specify error handling for git command failures in the `rebar verify-commit-ticket --rev HEAD` step itself (e.g., HEAD ref doesn't exist, detached HEAD with no commit, git not available) nor for `--message-file

` when the path is absent — the CLI spec only covers ticket-resolution outcomes, not I/O or subprocess errors.
findings.location: AC: CLI `rebar verify-commit-ticket` — input modes; CI: `gerrit-verify.yaml` `require-ticket` job
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the CLI AC: 'If `git show` exits non-zero, print a DISTINGUISHABLE infra-error (not a missing-ticket error) and exit 1. If `--message-file` path does not exist or is unreadable, print a clear usage error and exit 2.' Add corresponding test cases to `tests/unit/test_verify_commit_ticket.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the CI `require-ticket` job step an explicit `env: REBAR_TRACKER_DIR: .tickets-tracker` (or a `-c tracker.dir=.tickets-tracker` flag) so `config.tracker_dir()` resolves to the mounted worktree path regardless of what the PR under test sets in `rebar.toml`.
findings.finding: The CI `require-ticket` job mounts the store at `.tickets-tracker` via `git worktree add --detach .tickets-tracker FETCH_HEAD` but the plan never specifies setting `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR=.tickets-tracker` in the job step. `config.tracker_dir()` resolves via env > `rebar.toml` `tracker.dir` > default `.tickets-tracker`. This works by coincidence of default today, but a future PR that modifies `tracker.dir` in `rebar.toml` would cause the gate to silently look up a wrong (possibly empty) path — `resolve_ticket_id` would return `None` for every ID, the check would fail on a bogus 'no ticket' error rather than for the right reason, or (if `verify.require_ticket_for_commit` stays false for that run) pass silently.
findings.location: CI section — 'run `rebar verify-commit-ticket --rev HEAD`' step
findings.suggested_fix: Add `env:\n  REBAR_TRACKER_DIR: .tickets-tracker` to the `rebar verify-commit-ticket` step in the `require-ticket` job, pinning the store path independently of any config file changes in the patchset.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an end-to-end proving step for the CI job path — either a `workflow_dispatch` test commit with a missing/valid ticket (documented as a manual smoke-test AC), or a rationale for why the local commands are sufficient substitutes for the live GitHub Actions environment.
findings.finding: The Verification section has no proving command for the CI pipeline path (the `require-ticket` job in gerrit-verify.yaml). Since `rebar.toml` enables `verify.require_ticket_for_commit = true` for this project, the new code path is active in production CI — but the only proving commands are local/offline (`rebar verify-commit-ticket --message ...`), which bypass the store-mounting retry logic and the full job wiring.
findings.location: Verification section; CI AC ('gerrit-verify.yaml gains a require-ticket job')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fetch timeout value (e.g. `timeout 30 git fetch --depth=1 …`) in the CI section so the executor does not have to choose arbitrarily.
findings.finding: The CI job specifies 'fetch with a timeout' but does not state the concrete timeout value. An executor must choose a number without guidance.
findings.location: CI section — 'fetch with a timeout and up to 3 attempts'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `timeout 30` (seconds) to the fetch command in the CI step description, e.g. `timeout 30 git fetch --depth=1 https://github.com/${{ github.repository }} tickets`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit post-job cleanup step (e.g., `git worktree remove --force .tickets-tracker || true`) or move the `rm -rf .tickets-tracker && git worktree prune` to an `if: always()` post-step so the worktree is deregistered even on failure, preventing accumulation on persistent runners.
findings.finding: The `.tickets-tracker` git worktree is never explicitly removed after the `require-ticket` job completes; the only cleanup is `git worktree prune` at the START of the next run. On persistent (non-ephemeral) self-hosted runners, or if a second workflow run starts on the same workspace before the worktree directory is pruned, `git worktree add --detach .tickets-tracker FETCH_HEAD` will fail because the path already exists and prune won't remove a still-registered worktree.
findings.location: CI: `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` — `require-ticket` job, mount-tickets-store step
findings.suggested_fix:
Add after the `rebar verify-commit-ticket` run step:
```yaml
- name: cleanup-tickets-worktree
  if: always()
  run: git worktree remove --force .tickets-tracker 2>/dev/null || true
```
This ensures the worktree is deregistered regardless of job outcome, making the prune-at-start step redundant but harmless as a belt-and-suspenders guard.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale in the Decisions section for the worktree-fetch approach to CI store mounting (e.g., '`resolve_ticket_id` requires a real directory tree for its `os.listdir`/`os.path.isdir` scan; a full worktree is the simplest way to provide that without a full clone').
findings.finding: The plan's Decisions section states a positive rationale for the trailer-vs-first-element choice, but provides no positive rationale for the CI store-mounting approach (git fetch + worktree) itself — why this over alternatives such as an artifact-download cache, a sparse checkout of the tickets branch, or direct `git show tickets:path` reads. The 'why it fits' for the core CI data-access mechanism is absent.
findings.location: Decisions section; CI section — 'mount the authoritative tickets store from GitHub'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Decisions section, add: '**CI store mount:** `git worktree add` is chosen over `git archive` / `git show` because `resolve_ticket_id` requires a real filesystem directory tree (it calls `os.listdir(tracker_dir)` for prefix/alias scans); a bare object-db or tar stream cannot satisfy that requirement without additional extraction.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test scenario (and specify the behavior) for `verify_commit_message` when `tracker_dir()` is absent or empty — assert it fails closed (exit 1) with a DISTINGUISHABLE infra-error message, not an unhandled exception or a false 'no ticket found' error.
findings.finding: The behavior of `verify_commit_message` (and the CLI) when `tracker_dir()` points at a missing, empty, or corrupt store is unspecified and untested. The CI job mounts a live worktree on every run; if that mount is stale, absent, or malformed (e.g. partial fetch), the command could raise an unhandled exception rather than failing closed with a clear error, making the infra failure indistinguishable from a legitimate 'ticket not found' failure.
findings.location: Tests AC — `tests/unit/test_verify_commit_ticket.py` / `verify_commit_message` resolution logic
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `test_verify_commit_ticket.py`: a test that calls `verify_commit_message` (or the CLI via `--message`) with `root` pointing at a temp dir containing no tickets store, and asserts (a) `ok=False`, (b) the detail string contains an 'infra' or 'store unavailable' token distinct from the missing-ticket message. In `verify_commit.py`, catch `FileNotFoundError`/`OSError` from `resolve_ticket_id` and return `(False, tried, 'store unavailable: <path> — recheck')`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (via `git log --grep='rebar-ticket:'` on the Gerrit repo) that the trailer actually appears on recent commits, or correct the plan's Context section to say 'the convention is being introduced by this work' rather than 'it appears on recent commits'.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'A `rebar-ticket:` commit trailer is introduced by this work as the convention (it appears on recent commits but nothing parses it yet)' — implying the trailer is already in use on commits. A full-repo grep for `rebar-ticket:` finds zero occurrences in any source file, and no git log is readable at review time. This unverifiable premise about prior adoption affects the framing of the rollout narrative and the risk of the trailer being absent on the very first submitted commits.
findings.location: Context / Why — 'A `rebar-ticket:` commit trailer is introduced by this work as the convention (it appears on recent commits but nothing parses it yet)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'it appears on recent commits but nothing parses it yet' to 'this plan introduces the convention; no existing commits carry it yet' — or, if the trailer genuinely already exists in the repo history, add a verification step (`git log --grep='rebar-ticket:' --oneline | head`) to the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the CI job step how the CLI locates the mounted store — add an explicit `env: REBAR_TRACKER_DIR: .tickets-tracker` (or show the equivalent `tracker.dir` override) so `config.tracker_dir()` resolves to the worktree path.
findings.finding: The CI job mounts the tickets store at `.tickets-tracker` via `git worktree add --detach .tickets-tracker FETCH_HEAD` but never shows the job setting `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR=.tickets-tracker` (or any equivalent). The CLI uses `config.tracker_dir(root)` whose default comes from config/toml, not the worktree path — so `rebar verify-commit-ticket` would resolve against the wrong (or absent) store location in CI unless this env var is explicitly set in the workflow step.
findings.location: CI section — 'mount the authoritative tickets store' + 'run rebar verify-commit-ticket --rev HEAD'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `require-ticket` job's `rebar verify-commit-ticket` step: `env:\n  REBAR_TRACKER_DIR: .tickets-tracker`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the CI job's `require-ticket` step, explicitly bridge the mounted store path to the CLI — either prepend `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR=.tickets-tracker` to the `rebar verify-commit-ticket` invocation, OR document in the Store Resolution AC that `rebar.toml` already sets `tracker.dir = ".tickets-tracker"` so the default config resolves correctly.
findings.finding: The CI job mounts the tickets store at `.tickets-tracker` via `git worktree add --detach .tickets-tracker FETCH_HEAD`, but the subsequent `rebar verify-commit-ticket --rev HEAD` invocation never sets `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR=.tickets-tracker` (or any equivalent mechanism). The Store Resolution AC says the store is located via `config.tracker_dir(root)`, which reads from config/env. If the project's `rebar.toml` `tracker.dir` default does not happen to equal `.tickets-tracker`, the CLI will silently look in the wrong location and produce a false-negative (or error) instead of resolving against the mounted store.
findings.location: CI AC (`require-ticket` job) vs. Store Resolution AC
findings.suggested_fix: Change the CI run step to: `env:\n  REBAR_TRACKER_DIR: .tickets-tracker\nrun: rebar verify-commit-ticket --rev HEAD`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `CLAUDE.md` edit target: the section `## Git workflow (code changes)` does not exist in the file — specify which existing section should receive the git-trailer guidance (e.g., the parallel-agent workflow section or a new section to be created), or remove the section reference.
findings.finding: `CLAUDE.md` has no `## Git workflow (code changes)` section. The plan specifies adding content 'in the `## Git workflow (code changes)` section' of `CLAUDE.md`, but searching the file confirms no such heading exists — the file has sections like '## The parallel-agent workflow' and agent-workflow prose, but no 'Git workflow' heading.
findings.location: AC: Docs — `CLAUDE.md` — 'in the `## Git workflow (code changes)` section'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `permissions: contents: read` block to the `require-ticket` job in `gerrit-verify.yaml` so the job's IAM scope is pinned, portable, and least-privilege regardless of org-level defaults.
findings.finding: The new `require-ticket` CI job does not declare a `permissions:` block. GitHub Actions jobs inherit workflow-level or organization-level default permissions, which may be either overly broad (write-all) or too restrictive (preventing the `git fetch` from authenticating). Without an explicit least-privilege grant (e.g., `permissions: contents: read`), the job's actual IAM surface is undefined and non-reproducible across different org configurations.
findings.location: CI section — `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` `require-ticket` job
findings.suggested_fix:
```yaml
require-ticket:
  permissions:
    contents: read
  needs: [clear-vote]
  runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  steps:
    ...
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the `require-ticket` job's store-mount commands with the proven repo pattern: use `git fetch origin '+tickets:refs/remotes/origin/tickets' --depth=1` (or the equivalent HTTPS URL with explicit refspec mapping) and `git worktree add -B tickets .tickets-tracker origin/tickets`, matching `reconcile-bridge.yml` lines 102–104.
findings.finding: The CI `require-ticket` job's store-mounting git commands diverge from the proven working pattern in the repo and contain a technical gap: `git fetch --depth=1 https://github.com/${{ github.repository }} tickets` without a refspec mapping (`+tickets:refs/remotes/origin/tickets`) stores the result only in `FETCH_HEAD`, and the subsequent `git worktree add --detach .tickets-tracker FETCH_HEAD` is not the pattern the repo uses. The working pattern (reconcile-bridge.yml lines 102–104) is `git fetch origin '+tickets:refs/remotes/origin/tickets' --depth=1` then `git worktree add -B tickets .tickets-tracker origin/tickets`. The plan's variant is likely to fail: after `checkout-gerrit-change-action`, the remote is set to the GitHub mirror URL, and a bare `git fetch <url> tickets` without the `+src:dst` refspec doesn't guarantee `FETCH_HEAD` points to the correct commit for `worktree add`.
findings.location: CI AC — `require-ticket` job, store-mount steps
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the plan's fetch+worktree sequence with:
```
git fetch --depth=1 https://github.com/${{ github.repository }} '+refs/heads/tickets:refs/remotes/origin/tickets'
rm -rf .tickets-tracker
git worktree add -B tickets .tickets-tracker refs/remotes/origin/tickets
```
This matches the refspec pattern already proven in reconcile-bridge.yml.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `env: REBAR_TRACKER_DIR: .tickets-tracker` (or an absolute path) to the `rebar verify-commit-ticket` CI step so the CLI resolves the store to the mounted worktree, not the checked-out Gerrit source tree.
findings.finding: The CI `require-ticket` job never explicitly sets `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR=.tickets-tracker` (or equivalent) before invoking `rebar verify-commit-ticket --rev HEAD`. After `checkout-gerrit-change-action` checks out the Gerrit patchset, `config.tracker_dir()` will look for `.tickets-tracker` under the checked-out working tree root. The working tree is the Gerrit change's source tree (which will not contain `.tickets-tracker` because that branch is separately managed). Without the env var, resolution will always fail with 'store not found', producing Verified-1 for every change.
findings.location: CI AC — `require-ticket` job, `rebar verify-commit-ticket` invocation step
findings.suggested_fix: In the `run: rebar verify-commit-ticket --rev HEAD` step

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
